Its a little tricky to use at 1st, but it works, and its fairly good for getting on paper, I used it at the range, which was nice to help get on paper 1st.... when I sighted in scoped mosin on center line it hit high at 100 yards, (like 14 inches) so I went 2 squares up from center at first, then shot, still high but better, then went 4 squares above, and made 6 shots in 6 inch circle at 100 yards, the rifle was rested with sand bags and I barely touched the gun to fire, just to pull trigger and just having my shoulder against it.    Title: Zeroing in
   Star rating: 80%
   Date posted: 4/19/23
   Full review text: Watched a retired lifetime gunsmith use one to boresight a rifle/scope project of mine -- a semi-auto which could not be boresighted the traditional way. Sure enough, he got me very close at 100 yards. I researched it, the price was reasonable, and so I bought one for myself. What truly surprised me was that my brand new one seems to be right there in quality comparison with the smiths 20+ year old unit. Nice case, decently built, the biggest difference being that -- despite what the product photo depicts above -- the cases now come with a weird magnetic latching system for the flap. Seems cheesy, but works well.....so far. My biggest squawk? It isn't very close to true zero, but at least it is consistent. Once I figured out that it would always put the first shot three inches high and two left, I could adjust the scopes reticles against the internal collimating grid to compensate and get a near-bullseye on the first shot thereafter. This will definitely get you close to zero at 100 yards on your first shot thus saving precious ammo at the range the next day. I also own a laser type boresighter and this Bushnell seems to be a better choice. The lasers are all so stinking dim that they can only be used outdoors at dusk and at very close ranges, whereas this Bushnell unit can be used indoors from the comfort of your own living room and no scrambling for oddball batteries at the last minute.

Placed the gauge in the barrel with the handle against the flash hider, the O ring compressed in the mouth of the flash hider preventing the gauge from rotating. I was then able to easily bore sight the rifle, and duplicate the results several times. The result of the bore sighting placed the shots near the bullseye for an easy zero.
